content.assessment: This proposal explicitly uses demographic decline as political cover for contraction, which aligns it directly with the counterfactual trajectory already underway. Capacity caps and score thresholds applied against a backdrop of accelerating cohort decline (record-low 2024 births, further 7.1% fall in 2025, TFR at 1.31) are pushing in the same direction as demographic forces would push unassisted, raising the question of what the policy adds beyond timing and control over where contraction falls. The proposal's advantage over pure passive contraction is that it can target capacity reduction where the evidence shows selectivity is concentrated (urban/Budapest) rather than leaving rural provision to bear the brunt, potentially correcting the geographically uneven pattern the counterfactual would otherwise produce.
opportunities: Ministerial discretionary approval power could be calibrated using regional demographic data to prioritize contraction where cohort decline is steepest and viability already most compromised
strengths: Explicitly recognizes and instrumentalizes demographic decline rather than treating it as background noise, aligning policy timing with an accelerating structural trend
Capacity caps could be designed to target urban oversubscribed flagship tracks, addressing the geographic unevenness that passive contraction alone would leave uncorrected
threats: Church and foundation schools sit outside the demographic-driven consolidation pressure affecting state-sector Klebelsberg schools and could absorb displaced demand, meaning the net contraction achieved may be smaller than the state-sector numbers suggest once sector-shifting is accounted for
weaknesses: Statutory minimum class-size thresholds already create mechanical pressure toward contraction independent of this policy, so the marginal demographic contribution of active caps versus the counterfactual is unclear without further specification

Does not engage with the finding that rural viability challenges are partly independent of national cohort trends (driven by regional-specific contraction of 10-30%), so uniform national thresholds may hit rural and urban tracks asymmetrically
